// 01. About

Engineering at the
Silicon Edge

SEIF HUSSEIN ALSHABORY
EMBEDDED & AUTOMOTIVE ENG.

I'm Seif Hussein Alshabory, an Embedded Systems & Automotive Engineering student with a passion for bridging hardware and software. I build intelligent systems using microcontrollers like PIC, AVR, and ESP32 — from sensor-driven control loops to full smart-home automation platforms.

My work spans bare-metal C/C++ firmware, real-time sensor integration, wireless communication (WiFi/Bluetooth via ESP32), pneumatic control systems, and IoT-connected embedded solutions.

I love turning complex engineering problems into clean, efficient embedded code — where every instruction counts and hardware constraints demand creative thinking.

// 02. Experience

Hands-On
Field Work

Automotive Electrical Diagnostics
Field Experience
Car Electrical Systems // Practical Diagnostics
Hands-on experience diagnosing and troubleshooting automotive electrical systems. Skilled in reading wiring diagrams and tracing faults across complex vehicle electrical architectures — from starter and charging circuits to lighting, sensors, and ECU communication lines. Proficient with OBD-II scanners for reading and clearing fault codes, live data streaming, and sensor output analysis.
OBD-II Diagnostics Fault Code Reading Wiring Diagrams Multimeter Testing CAN Bus Analysis ECU Fault Tracing Battery & Alternator Sensor Testing
Mechanical Automotive Knowledge
Technical Knowledge
Vehicle Mechanical Systems // Engine & Drivetrain
Strong foundational knowledge of internal combustion engine operation, drivetrain components, and vehicle mechanical systems. Understanding of how mechanical subsystems — engine timing, fuel delivery, cooling, and braking — interact with electronic control units, enabling a full-stack view from metal to microcontroller.
Engine Systems Fuel Injection Cooling Systems Transmission Brake Systems Ignition Systems Mechanical–ECU Link Vehicle Inspection
Embedded Systems Projects
Academic & Personal
Self-Directed Engineering // GitHub Portfolio
Designed and built multiple embedded systems projects from concept to working prototype — including IoT smart-home platforms, temperature-controlled actuator systems, and automated pneumatic storage controllers. All projects are open-source and available on GitHub.
PIC / AVR / ESP32 C / C++ Circuit Design Proteus Simulation IoT Integration Open Source

// CASE STUDY 01
PIC-Based Intelligent Temperature Management
C / MikroC PROPIC16F877A LM35ADCPWM
Overview

An automated temperature monitoring and control system built around the PIC16F877A microcontroller. It integrates an analog LM35 sensor with a digital interface and actuators to maintain safe thermal parameters through a tiered response protocol.

System Architecture
Technical Architecture
MicrocontrollerPIC16F877A (8-bit, 20MHz)
Temperature SensorLM35 — Analog, 10mV/°C output
Signal ProcessingADC conversion → Temp°C = V(mv) / 10
Actuator 1DC Motor (Cooling Fan) — PWM speed control
Actuator 2Tri-color LED — Green / Yellow / Red alerts
SoftwareBare-metal C, MikroC PRO compiler
Safety Protocol3-tier: Safe (<30°C) / Warning (30–45°C) / Critical (>45°C)
Key Challenge

Problem: Raw ADC readings from the LM35 were noisy, causing the fan to rapidly switch on and off (chattering) near threshold temperatures, which risks motor damage and poor UX.

Solution: Implemented a hysteresis band of ±2°C around each threshold. The system only transitions between states when the temperature crosses the threshold by this margin, eliminating chattering entirely and stabilizing the control loop.

View Source Code →
// CASE STUDY 02
Multi-Node AVR Smart Home Ecosystem
CATmega32 ×2 SPIUARTPWM
Overview

A decentralized home automation system using a Master/Slave ATmega32 configuration. The Master handles security authentication and fire detection, while the Slave manages climate and lighting via SPI inter-node communication. User interaction is via UART.

System Architecture
Technical Architecture
MicrocontrollersDual ATmega32 (AVR) — Master/Slave topology
Inter-node Comm.SPI — Master dispatches commands to Slave
User InterfaceUART — Serial login and command input
Security LayerCredential-based login, 3-attempt lockout
Door ControlPWM-driven Servo motor
Signal QualityMoving Average Filter on analog sensor readings
SafetyFire Alarm with automatic ventilation trigger
Key Challenge

Problem: The Master node was becoming overloaded handling both security events (login, fire alarm) and environmental control simultaneously, causing delayed response to critical security triggers.

Solution: Offloaded all lighting and climate logic to the Slave node via SPI, freeing the Master to dedicate its cycles entirely to time-critical security events. This distributed architecture ensures the Master always responds to intrusion or fire events with zero delay regardless of environmental workload.

Project Photos
View Source Code →
// CASE STUDY 03
Hybrid IoT Smart Home V6 (Arduino & ESP32)
C++ESP32 Blynk IoTFingerprintRFID
Overview

A professional-grade IoT ecosystem that cleanly separates mission-critical security from environmental automation. The ESP32 handles biometrics, RFID, cloud sync, and the Blynk mobile dashboard, while the Arduino Uno manages all hardware I/O, bridged via a JSON-based UART protocol with hardware voltage divider for 5V→3.3V signal integrity.

System Architecture
Technical Architecture
IoT GatewayESP32 — WiFi, Blynk cloud integration
SecurityR307 Fingerprint + RFID RC522 + Keypad (3-factor)
IPC ProtocolJSON-based UART between ESP32 and Arduino Uno
Signal IntegrityVoltage Divider (5V→3.3V) for UART level shifting
TelemetryReal-time Smoke, Temp, Humidity → Blynk mobile dashboard
SafetyAuto fire mitigation + 30-second lockout on failed auth
Hardware I/OArduino Uno handles all relay switching and sensor reading
Key Challenge

Problem: Direct UART connection between the 3.3V ESP32 and 5V Arduino caused signal integrity issues and risked permanent damage to the ESP32's GPIO pins.

Solution: Designed a resistive voltage divider circuit on the Arduino TX line to safely step down the 5V signal to 3.3V before feeding it to the ESP32 RX pin. Combined with a JSON-structured protocol over UART, this created a robust and electrically-safe inter-processor communication bridge.

Project Photos
View Source Code →
// CASE STUDY 04
Automated Storage & Retrieval System (ASRS)
C++Arduino Mega 2560 Stepper MotorPneumaticsUltrasonic
Overview

An industrial-grade 3×3 warehouse automation prototype. The system manages item storage and retrieval using coordinate-based motion control across three axes — X (stepper), Y (pneumatic lift), Z (pneumatic pusher) — with a 9-node ultrasonic matrix for real-time occupancy tracking.

System Architecture
Technical Architecture
Control HubArduino Mega 2560
X-Axis MotionStepper Motor — Trapezoidal acceleration profile
Y-Axis MotionPneumatic Lift — Vertical item elevation
Z-Axis MotionPneumatic Pusher — Item ejection into slot
Occupancy Sensing9-node Ultrasonic matrix (3×3 grid coverage)
End-Stop DetectionIR limit switches on all axes
Autonomous ModeAuto-Scan: algorithmically finds free slot & stores item
Key Challenge

Problem: Moving a loaded carriage on the X-axis with a constant velocity stepper profile caused mechanical vibration and occasional step-loss under load, making positioning unreliable for precise slot alignment.

Solution: Implemented a trapezoidal motion profile — the stepper accelerates gradually from rest, cruises at target speed, then decelerates before stopping. This eliminated inertial shock, prevented step-loss, and achieved reliable ±1mm positioning accuracy across all 3 columns of the storage grid.

Project Photos
View Source Code →
